query: recent studies on the effectiveness of ultrasound vs MRI in diagnosing internal derangement of the temporomandibular joint

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the preferred imaging study to assess the TMJ due to its capacity to accurately show joint effusion, disc displacement, and soft tissues. [15] However, a simple panoramic X-ray can help rapidly evaluate the state of the dentition and joint and tends to be the initial investigation. Plain radiographs and CT scans can show severe joint degeneration, fractures, and dislocations. [1] Ultrasonography shows the disc position, but it does not help diagnose osteoarthritis. [1] Computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are more beneficial in severe, chronic, or suspected structural abnormalities of TMJ. [16] [17] Additionally, newer techniques of nerve blocks, botulinum toxin injections, arthrography, and mandibular motion data can prove to be of significant diagnostic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ)
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with TMJ symptoms suffer from internal disc derangement or myofascial pain, where radiography does not tend to provide extra helpful information. [8] CBCT is effective in detecting condylar osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. [8] MRI is the method of choice when the soft tissues of the TMJ are to be evaluated. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: 3-3-2 Rule -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound presents potential advantages over traditional methods for measuring condylar mobility and TMD (tongue thickness, TT) for airway assessment. The undiagnosed cases of temporomandibular disorders during surgery are mentioned, highlighting the limitations of the maximal mouth-opening method for assessing TMJ mobility.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Clinical Significance -- Instrumental Assessments
retrieved_docs.content: Instrumental assessments are essential for accurate diagnosis and should be performed before initiating any therapy for TMJ dysfunction. [19] Panoramic radiography allows evaluation of the relationship between the mandibular condyles and the corresponding glenoid fossae. Multislice or cone-beam computed tomography enables detailed assessment of the morphology of TMJ components, bone position, and potential pathologies. Magnetic resonance imaging provides visualization of the articular disc, as well as joint anatomy, function, and form.

retrieved_docs.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging Patient Positioning -- Issues of Concern -- Musculoskeletal Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) The TMJ coils have 2 pieces connected by a holder. With the patient supine, the coil's loop must be placed at the TMJ and as close as possible to the patient's face without causing discomfort. If no TMJ coils are available, routine brain coils can be used.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Facial Muscle Energy, Direct MFR, and BLT Procedure – for TMJ Dysfunction -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Key landmarks derive from the temporal bone and mandible. Areas to recognize on the temporal bone palpation include the mastoid, parietal notch, and zygomatic processes. Important non-palpable landmarks include the external acoustic meatus, occipital mastoid suture, spheno-squamous pivot, mandibular fossa, and eustachian tube. Palpable areas to recognize on the mandible include the ramus, angle, body, articular eminence, and mental protuberance. Non-palpable areas that remain clinically important include the condylar process, articular cartilage, medial and lateral collateral ligaments, stylomandibular ligament, and sphenomandibular ligament. Internally, the lateral temporomandibular ligament and joint capsule balance the sphenomandibular ligament. Inferiorly, the mandible angle is connected to the styloid process by the stylomandibular ligament, which contracts and extends to allow for the hinge and gliding action, resulting in jaw opening and closing. When full jaw flexion (opening) is displayed on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), the region resembles a "bow-tie" with the articular disc seeded inside the anterior condyle at its mid-point. With jaw-closing or extension, the disc's posterior aspect eventually lines perpendicular to the anterior condyle. Pathology typically presents as hyperextension, resulting in bite blocks that are advised for prevention. [4] [5] Many patients suffering from TMJ have an overbite, classified as Class II in the dental literature. Class I patients have little to no overnight, whereas class III is an underbite with lower teeth protrusion. While osteopathic manipulative treatment focuses on the joint itself, it also involves treating the muscles, which are also secondarily affected by joint dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.content: Knowledge regarding the effects of surgery in the presence of synovial plicae is limited. Functional alterations caused by the plica may remain asymptomatic and are often detected only through instrumental examination. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical evaluation of the TMJ begins with observation of natural mandibular movements. Assessment includes facial symmetry, the range and quality of TMJ movements, the onset and severity of pain during opening, closing, and accessory movements, and the presence of joint sounds.

retrieved_docs.title: 3-3-2 Rule -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The modified Mallampati scale (MMS) and Wilson score are identified as predictors of DI, although with limited sensitivity. In addition, ultrasound measurements of TT and condylar mobility also demonstrate correlations with DI. The role of condylar translation during laryngoscopy and the implications of reduced condylar mobility on the procedure are discussed.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mandible fracture and mandible condylar fracture are important to recognize early; they may occur in conjunction with mandibular dislocation. In patients with dislocation secondary to trauma, imaging should be performed to assess for fracture. Infections that may appear similar to TMJ dislocation can include epiglottitis, retropharyngeal abscess, or peritonsillar abscess. These infectious presentations can have similar clinical features to mandibular dislocation, including drooling, trismus, and throat or neck pain. It is important to evaluate the oropharynx and obtain an appropriate history from the patient as the treatment for infection and dislocation differ vastly. TMJ dysfunction or acute closed locking of the TMJ meniscus and dystonic reaction such as tetanus can be mistaken for dislocation as the patient will not be able to open their mouth normally. History, physical exam, and imaging are important for differentiating between the features of each disease from mandibular dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The TMJ performs complex movements across multiple orthogonal planes and axes of rotation, functioning synergistically with the other components (see Image . Temporomandibular Joint). Coordination with the contralateral TMJ ensures synchronized movement. Dysfunction or pathology of this structure can lead to pain, limited jaw movement, and impaired quality of life. Medical management focuses on diagnosing disorders such as TMJ dysfunction, arthritis, and trauma. Surgical intervention may be necessary for severe joint degeneration, ankylosis, tumors, or congenital anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: No initial laboratory testing is necessary for isolated TMJ dislocation. Women of childbearing age should have a pregnancy test performed before performing imaging.

[1] Evaluating the oral cavity, the inner ear (otoscopy), and palpating the neck musculature help exclude other causes of facial pain. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Palpation of the joint during mandibular opening enables the detection of additional abnormalities. With fingers supporting the TMJ, the patient is asked to perform tongue protrusion, rotation, neck flexion and extension, and lateral inclinations during mouth opening. These maneuvers help reveal accentuation of TMJ dysfunction in association with other musculoskeletal structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Autoradiografie, diagnostické techniky radioizotopové a diagnostické techniky otologické jsou všechny metodiky, u nichž lze uplatnit přístrojové vybavení jako povolený kvalifikátor. Stejně tak náhražky mléka, jiné respirační terapie, peritoneální dialýza a obecně i přístrojové vybavení samotné mohou být kvalifikovány pomocí přístrojového vybavení, což zahrnuje i obecnou anestezii (التخدير العام). Přístrojové vybavení může být dále specifikováno scintilací – počítáním a emisní počítačovou tomografií, čímž se rozšiřuje jeho využití v nukleární medicíně. Non‑imaging nuclear medicine probe or assay představuje centrální prvek, který je spojen s autoradiografií a má širší vztah k diagnostickým technikám radioizotopovým. Tento typ sondy nebo assay je součástí nukleární medicíny a je také propojen s scintilací – počítáním a emisní počítačovou tomografií. K jeho funkci jsou využívány radionuklidy jako látky, které jsou v tomto kontextu používány. Celkově tedy přístrojové vybavení slouží jako klíčový kvalifikátor pro širokou škálu diagnostických a terapeutických postupů – od autoradiografie a radioizotopových diagnostických technik po otologické vyšetření, peritoneální dialýzu, respirační terapii a obecnou anestezii – a je úzce propojeno s neobrazovými sondami a assayemi nukleární medicíny, které využívají radionuklidy a jsou podporovány metodami jako scintilace – počítání a emisní počítačová tomografie.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2875
retrieved_docs.content: Musculoskeletal disorder therapy is closely linked to the use of ultrasound therapy (المعالجة بموجات فوق صوتية). The modality of ultrasound (ultrazvuk) functions as an entry combination for this therapy, indicating that basic ultrasound application is incorporated into the treatment approach. Additionally, ultrasound surgery (ultrazvuková chirurgie) is related to the same ultrasound therapy, reflecting that therapeutic ultrasound can also be employed in surgical procedures. Together, these connections underscore the central role of ultrasound‑based techniques in managing musculoskeletal disorders, both as a non‑invasive therapeutic modality and as a component of surgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 559
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge organizes medical imaging and nuclear medicine into a hierarchical framework. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and diagnostic ultrasound are classified under the imaging/visualization/scanning domain, linked to cross‑sectional anatomy and biomedical imaging, and both require appropriate device‑equipment qualifiers. General radiology procedures, techniques and services serve as a parent category for MRI and for heat‑map imaging, which likewise belongs to the imaging/visualization/scanning and bioimaging families. “Other diagnostic ultrasound” is a subcategory that encompasses diagnostic ultrasound; a specific child of this branch is ultrasonography of the head and neck. The broader grouping “Radiology, Radiotherapy, Nuclear Medicine and Ultrasound Procedures” includes diagnostic ultrasound and extends to non‑imaging nuclear medicine probes or assays, integrating conventional imaging with functional nuclear techniques.
